artifact.description: Fiscal and Economic Impact Assessment: SB-1247, Rent Stabilization Act — 38-page legislative impact assessment with economic modeling, comparative jurisdiction analysis, and equity impact analysis

ai_assistance.description: Synthesized findings from 23 published studies on rent stabilization; ran Monte Carlo simulations modeling housing supply impacts under three scenarios
Generated initial draft of comparative jurisdiction section from structured data; produced plain-language summaries of technical sections

review.notes: Division director review; requested sensitivity analysis, strengthened limitations section, moved comparative table to appendix
Deputy Secretary approved for release to legislature; requested AI disclosure cover letter

governance.policy: State Housing Agency AI Use and Transparency Policy

decisions.description: Selected presentation approach for housing supply impact finding: lead with uncertainty range (1.1%–6.8%), follow with central estimate (3.2%), contextualize against baseline trend (2.1% decline without legislation)
decisions.context: The supply impact finding is the most politically consequential number in the assessment. Framing determines whether legislators interpret the bill as moderately or severely affecting housing supply. Three presentation approaches were drafted and compared.

aiInputs.contribution: Produced the Monte Carlo simulation results: central estimate of 3.2% reduction with 90% CI of 1.1%–6.8%. AI generated the numbers but had no role in framing or interpretation.

decisions.rationale: Leading with the range communicates genuine uncertainty to legislators without burying the best estimate. Following immediately with the central estimate ensures the most likely outcome is clearly stated. Contextualizing against the baseline trend prevents the finding from being interpreted in isolation. Division director concurred with this approach. Alternative framings (leading with central estimate alone, or leading with baseline comparison) were considered and rejected as either overstating precision or obscuring the finding.

decisions.description: Decided to author the equity impact section entirely without AI assistance
decisions.context: The equity impact section examines effects on low-income renters, small landlords, and communities of color using census microdata, tenant surveys, and fair housing enforcement data.
decisions.rationale: AI-generated analysis of equity impacts carries unacceptable risk of embedding unexamined assumptions about race, income, and housing markets. The section requires close reading of disaggregated data and contextual judgment that cannot be validated by checking AI output against source material — unlike the literature synthesis, there is no external reference to check against. The reputational and policy risk of an equity analysis that reflects algorithmic bias outweighs the time savings of AI assistance.

decisions.description: Rejected AI model's default landlord exit rate assumption and adjusted upward by 15% based on state-specific landlord survey data
decisions.context: Initial Monte Carlo simulation used national-average landlord exit rates. This state has a higher share of small landlords (2–4 unit buildings) who are more sensitive to rent regulation than institutional landlords.

aiInputs.contribution: Ran initial simulation with default national-average parameters; returned probability distributions that Fatima identified as underestimating small-landlord sensitivity

decisions.rationale: The state's most recent landlord survey shows 62% of rental units are in buildings with fewer than 5 units, compared to the national average of 48%. Small landlords have historically exited regulated markets at higher rates. Using the national average would systematically underestimate the supply effect in this state.

entities.label: Curated evidence base: 23 peer-reviewed studies and 5 state post-enactment reports
Simulation parameters: state housing data, BLS CPI forecasts, demographic projections, adjusted landlord exit rates
Structured comparative data from OR, CA, NY, MN, and Ontario
SB-1247 bill text and committee request
State Housing Agency, Division of Housing Policy

activities.description: Fatima curated evidence base: reviewed candidate studies, applied inclusion criteria (post-2015, peer-reviewed or government-issued, transparent methodology), excluded three industry-funded studies lacking disclosed assumptions
AI synthesized findings from 23 studies into structured comparison table

interaction.responseNotes: Corrected two extraction errors: one misattributed finding, one truncated limitation. Added contextual notes on four studies requiring adjustment for policy design differences.

activities.description: AI ran initial Monte Carlo simulation (10,000 iterations x 3 scenarios) with default national-average parameters

interaction.responseNotes: Fatima identified that landlord exit rate assumption used national averages not reflective of this state's higher small-landlord share. Rejected results and adjusted parameter upward by 15%.

activities.description: AI reran Monte Carlo simulation with state-adjusted landlord exit rate parameter

interaction.responseNotes: Results accepted after Fatima verified distributions against state-specific benchmarks. Central estimate: 3.2% supply reduction, 90% CI: 1.1%–6.8%.

activities.description: Fatima decided how to present the supply impact finding — chose to lead with range, follow with central estimate, contextualize against baseline
AI generated comparative jurisdiction section from Fatima's structured data

interaction.responseNotes: Substantially revised: reordered jurisdictions to lead with most comparable cases (OR, MN); rewrote Ontario comparison to note healthcare system differences; removed one sentence implying causation where evidence supports only correlation; added original analytical synthesis paragraph.

activities.description: Fatima authored equity impact section entirely without AI assistance, using census microdata, agency tenant survey, and fair housing enforcement data. Reviewed by agency civil rights liaison.
AI generated plain-language summaries of economic modeling and comparative jurisdiction sections

interaction.responseNotes: Corrected one instance where simplification changed meaning of a confidence interval. Rewrote equity section summary by hand.

activities.description: David Oluwale reviewed complete draft; requested sensitivity analysis, strengthened limitations section, moved comparative table to appendix
AI reran simulation with CPI +/- 1 percentage point for sensitivity analysis requested by division director

interaction.responseNotes: Results accepted. Sensitivity range documented in appendix. Central estimate shifts by approximately 0.4 percentage points in either direction.

activities.description: Margaret Liu reviewed assessment and approved for release; requested AI disclosure cover letter

relationships.notes: Institutional attribution — released as an agency document
Fatima provided curated studies and corrected two extraction errors
Fatima defined scenarios and parameters; rejected results due to inapplicable assumptions
Fatima adjusted landlord exit rate parameter and validated corrected results
Corrected run informed by rejection of initial run's assumptions
Division director consulted on framing approach and concurred
Fatima provided structured data and substantially revised the AI draft
Reviewed and corrected AI-generated summaries; wrote equity summary by hand
Sensitivity analysis was requested by division director during review
Fatima acts as the agency's analyst; the assessment is an institutional product
Evidence base section incorporates corrected synthesis
Economic modeling section reports simulation findings
Comparative section derived from AI draft but substantially rewritten

policyEvaluations.policy: State Housing Agency AI Use and Transparency Policy
policyEvaluations.notes: AI assistance disclosed in cover letter signed by Deputy Secretary. AI used only for research synthesis, simulation, and drafting — not for analytical conclusions, equity analysis, or recommendations. All AI outputs reviewed and corrected by analyst. Compliant with agency policy sections 3.1 (disclosure), 3.4 (human accountability), and 3.7 (sensitive content exclusion).

policyEvaluations.policy: State Freedom of Information Act — AI Transparency Requirements
policyEvaluations.notes: Assessment methodology section documents AI tool usage. HALOS provenance record provides machine-readable detail sufficient to respond to FOIA requests regarding AI involvement in the assessment's preparation. Cover letter provides public-facing disclosure.

Human authorship confirmed for all analytical conclusions, equity analysis, and executive summary. AI assistance properly disclosed and bounded. Decision provenance recorded for all material framing and methodology choices. Three-tier human review chain (analyst, director, deputy secretary) documented.
